title: Statistical Computing - Assignment 2
text:
This is the home page for my Quarto project for Assignment 2. The practical for this assignment can be found below:

Practical 12

title: Practical 12
text:
Setup

library(foreach) # uses %do%
library(doParallel) # uses %dopar%
library(MASS) # glalaxies data for Q2
library(iterators) # for Question 4 (irnorm)
library(tibble)
library(knitr)



Quetsion 1

cl <- makeCluster(3)
registerDoParallel(cl)

exp_mean_and_var <- foreach(i = 1:100, .combine = rbind) %dopar% {
    random_sample <- rexp(100,rate=1)
    sample_mean <- mean(random_sample)
    sample_variance <- var(random_sample)
    rbind(sample_mean,sample_variance)
    c(sample_mean, sample_variance)
}

stopCluster(cl)

colnames(exp_mean_and_var) <- c("Mean", "Variance")

Below are the first 10 rows of our resulting Mean and Variance for each of our 100 random samples:

exp_mean_and_var_tb <- as_tibble(exp_mean_and_var)
exp_mean_and_var_tb

# A tibble: 100 × 2
    Mean Variance
   <dbl>    <dbl>
 1 0.978    0.839
 2 0.853    0.734
 3 1.11     1.07 
 4 0.920    0.965
 5 1.13     1.67 
 6 0.946    0.834
 7 1.10     0.927
 8 1.13     1.28 
 9 1.11     1.19 
10 1.06     1.02 
# ℹ 90 more rows




Question 2

data <- galaxies
head(data)

[1]  9172  9350  9483  9558  9775 10227


Serial Processing:

system.time({
  galaxies_bs_medians <- foreach(i = 1:1000, .combine = c) %do% {
    bs_sample <- sample(galaxies,length(galaxies),replace = T)
    bs_median <- median(bs_sample)
}
})

   user  system elapsed 
   0.24    0.02    0.25 



hist(galaxies_bs_medians)








In parallel:

cl <- makeCluster(3)
registerDoParallel(cl)

system.time({
  galaxies_bs_medians <- foreach(i = 1:1000, .combine = c) %dopar% {
    bs_sample <- sample(data,length(data),replace = T)
    bs_median <- median(bs_sample)
}
})

   user  system elapsed 
   0.22    0.07    0.34 

stopCluster(cl)

The serial processing time was approximately equal to the parallel processing time.
Now we will try doing 1000 bootstrap samples at a time:

cl <- makeCluster(3)
registerDoParallel(cl)

system.time({
  galaxies_bs_medians_1000 <- foreach(i = 1:3, .combine = c) %dopar% {
    bs_sample_1000 <- replicate(
      1000,median(sample(data,length(data),replace = T)))
    bs_sample_1000
}
})

   user  system elapsed 
   0.02    0.00    0.11 

stopCluster(cl)


hist(galaxies_bs_medians_1000)








In this case, despite computing 3000 bootstrap medians, the processing time was much faster.


Question 3
Coverage of a percentile bootstrap confidence interval for the median:

cl <- makeCluster(3)
registerDoParallel(cl)

sample <- rexp(n = 50, rate = 1)
sample_mean <- mean(sample)
n <- 1000

system.time({
  coverage_pci_bs <- foreach(i = 1:n, .combine = c) %dopar% {
    new_sample <- rexp(n = 50, rate = 1/sample_mean)
    bs_sample_1000 <- replicate(
      n,mean(sample(new_sample,length(new_sample),replace = T)))
    mean_quantiles <- quantile(bs_sample_1000,probs = c(0.025,0.975))
  coverage_check <- if (
    mean_quantiles[1] <= sample_mean & sample_mean <= mean_quantiles[2]) {1} 
  else {0}
  coverage_check
}
})

   user  system elapsed 
   0.45    0.09    8.94 

stopCluster(cl)

We now have the number of times our confidence interval contained the original sample mean out of 1000. Therefore, the coverage is as follows:

coverage <- sum(coverage_pci_bs)/n
coverage

[1] 0.941




Question 4

cl <- makeCluster(3)
registerDoParallel(cl)

set.seed(1234)

max_values <- foreach(vec = irnorm(5, count = 3),
                          .combine = c) %dopar% {
  max(vec)
}
stopCluster(cl)

The below 3 values are the maximum of each of the 3 vectors of 5 random variables:

max_values

[1] 1.0844412 0.5060559 0.9594941




Question 5

time_foreach <- system.time({
  set.seed(1234)
  cl <- makeCluster(3)
  registerDoParallel(cl)
  max_values_foreach <- foreach(vec = irnorm(5, count = 3),
                          .combine = c) %dopar% {
  max(vec)
}
})

stopCluster(cl)

max_values_foreach

[1] 1.0844412 0.5060559 0.9594941

time_foreach

   user  system elapsed 
   0.00    0.02    0.33 



time_parLapply <- system.time({
  set.seed(1234)
  cl <- makeCluster(3)
  registerDoParallel(cl)
  
  max_vec_func <- function(i){
    vec <- rnorm(5)
    max(vec)
  }
  
  max_values_parLapply <- parLapply(cl = cl, 1:3, max_vec_func)
  max_values_parLapply <- unlist(max_values_parLapply)
})

stopCluster(cl)

max_values_parLapply

[1] 1.4067074 0.8687848 0.7324748

time_parLapply

   user  system elapsed 
   0.02    0.03    0.31 



time_replicate <- system.time({
  set.seed(1234)
  max_values_replicate <- replicate(3,max(rnorm(5)))
})

max_values_replicate

[1] 1.0844412 0.5060559 0.9594941

time_replicate

   user  system elapsed 
      0       0       0 


The table below shows shows the time it took each of the three above methods to run:

times <- rbind(time_foreach,time_parLapply,time_replicate)
kable(times[,1:3], caption = "Times Comparison of Three Methods")


Times Comparison of Three Methods



user.self
sys.self
elapsed




time_foreach
0.00
0.02
0.33


time_parLapply
0.02
0.03
0.31


time_replicate
0.00
0.00
0.00





From the table above, we can see that the foreach and parLapply functions have a similar run times, with no noticeable difference between them. Replicate, however, despite not running in parallel is much faster, practically instant. The time it takes to setup the clusters in foreach and parLapply make them slower for such a simple task. The benefit of parallelisation was not worth it.

max_values_all <- rbind(max_values_foreach,
                        max_values_parLapply,
                        max_values_replicate)

Below we compare the maximum values of each vector that each method produced:

kable(round(max_values_all,2), 
      caption = "Max Value Comparison of Three Methods")


Max Value Comparison of Three Methods


max_values_foreach
1.08
0.51
0.96


max_values_parLapply
1.41
0.87
0.73


max_values_replicate
1.08
0.51
0.96





From the table above, we can see that the use of irnorm in the foreach function resulted in the seed working correctly, as it matches the values achieved by replicate. Unfortunately, parLapply did not achieve the same values as each worker generates its own random numbers independently.
